title: Cervical Cancer
oneLiner: HPV-driven malignancy of cervix; presents with abnormal bleeding; preventable by screening, curable if detected early.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive haemorrhage** from tumour erosion into uterine/cervical vessels
  - *Single finding:* active heavy vaginal bleeding + haemodynamic instability (HR >110, SBP <90, capillary refill >2 s)
- **Bilateral ureteric obstruction** causing post-renal acute kidney injury
  - *Single finding:* anuria/oliguria + flank pain + palpable bladder (if complete obstruction) – or incidental raised creatinine + empty bladder on exam
- **Septic shock** from pyometra (infected obstructed endometrial cavity) or tumour necrosis
  - *Single finding:* fever + purulent vaginal discharge + hypotension – must differentiate from simple UTI
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Postcoital bleeding (most specific)
  - Intermenstrual bleeding / menorrhagia
  - Postmenopausal bleeding (always suspect until proven otherwise)
  - Persistent watery or offensive vaginal discharge
  - Deep dyspareunia
- **Later signs:**
  - Pelvic/back pain (parametrial invasion)
  - Unilateral lower limb oedema (lymphatic obstruction)
  - Haematuria or tenesmus (bladder/bowel invasion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Speculum: friable, exophytic or ulcerated cervical lesion; contact bleeding
  - Bimanual: bulky cervix, reduced mobility, nodular parametrium (fixed → advanced disease)
  - Inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (metastatic)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Cervical ectropion** – non-friable, red ring around os; no contact bleeding
- **Cervical polyp** – pedunculated, smooth, usually benign
- **Endometritis** – no visible lesion; pelvic tenderness, fever, discharge
- **Cervical fibroid** – firm, irregular but non-friable; usually asymptomatic
- **Metastatic disease** (e.g. endometrial, vaginal, bladder) – imaging ± biopsy needed
- **Splitter:** Friable lesion + contact bleeding on speculum → cervical cancer until proven; ectropion does not bleed on touch.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Vaginal speculum exam (visualise cervix)
  - Bimanual pelvic exam (assess stage)
  - Urinalysis (exclude UTI if sepsis suspected)
  - Bedside USS if obstructive symptoms (look for hydronephrosis)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, clotting, CRP
  - Group & save (if heavy bleeding)
  - Beta-hCG (rule out pregnancy – must do before any procedure)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** pelvic MRI (gold standard for local staging – tumour size, parametrial invasion, lymph nodes)
  - **Second-line:** CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (metastatic workup) or PET-CT if advanced
- **Special tests:**
  - Cervical biopsy (punch or LLETZ) – histology is diagnostic
  - HPV DNA testing (triage for colposcopy)
  - Colposcopy with acetic acid/Lugol’s iodine
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Histology shows squamous cell carcinoma (80%) or adenocarcinoma (20%). Staging via FIGO (clinical + imaging): stage I (confined to cervix) → IV (distant mets).
- **Never-miss test:** **Cervical biopsy** of any visible suspicious lesion – do not treat empirically. If no lesion but high suspicion, refer for colposcopy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Massive haemorrhage: call gynae-oncology ± interventional radiology; insert vaginal packing; IV fluids/blood; tranexamic acid; if unstable → emergency radiotherapy or embolisation
  - Suspected pyometra/sepsis: IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav + metronidazole [check local policy]), urgent drainage (dilatation & curettage ± drain)
  - Acute renal failure: catheterise (bladder decompression if retention); bilateral nephrostomy if obstruction confirmed
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Complete staging (MRI pelvis, CT CAP, PET if needed)
  - MDT referral (gynae-oncology, radiology, histopathology)
  - Discuss fertility-sparing options (early stage)
  - Start chemoradiation if advanced (within 4 weeks ideally)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Early stage (IA–IIA): surgery (cone biopsy, simple/extended hysterectomy) or radiotherapy
  - Locally advanced (IIB–IVA): concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin + external beam + brachytherapy)
  - Metastatic (IVB): palliative chemotherapy (cisplatin/carboplatin + paclitaxel ± bevacizumab)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: crystalloid 1 L/8h if maintaining hydration for cisplatin; otherwise oral + IV if needed
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal, unless bowel obstruction or post-procedure NBM
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; more frequent if unstable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (chemoradiation):**
  - Cisplatin 40 mg/m² IV weekly × 5–6 cycles (with pre/post hydration and antiemetics) – **[check local policy]**
  - For metastatic: carboplatin AUC 5 IV + paclitaxel 175 mg/m² IV q3w – **[check local policy]**
- **Haemorrhage:**
  - Tranexamic acid 1 g IV/PO TDS (max 4 g/day)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Cisplatin: avoid if eGFR <40 mL/min (or reduce dose); ensure Mg²+, K+, Ca²+ replete; contraindicated in pregnancy
  - Paclitaxel: premedicate with dexamethasone + antihistamine (anaphylaxis risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cisplatin dose reduction if eGFR <60; consider carboplatin if worse
  - No significant hepatic adjustment for platinum agents
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Cisplatin – **give adequate hydration (1 L normal saline before and after)** to prevent acute kidney injury; never give without checking recent U&E; ototoxicity is irreversible – monitor audiology if high cumulative dose
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Heavy vaginal bleeding** → hypovolaemic shock
  - **Ureteric obstruction** → acute kidney injury (may need nephrostomy)
  - **Pyometra/sepsis** → septic shock
  - **Fistula** (vesicovaginal / rectovaginal) – especially after radiotherapy
- **Chronic:**
  - **Lymphoedema** (lower limb, genital) – due to nodal involvement or radiotherapy
  - **Radiation proctitis/cystitis** – bleeding, tenesmus, frequency
  - **Sexual dysfunction** – vaginal stenosis, dyspareunia, reduced libido
  - **Infertility** – if hysterectomy or pelvic radiation
sections.Prognosis: - **Stage-dependent:** stage I 5-year survival >90%; stage II 60–80%; stage III 40–50%; stage IV <20%. Screening (smear + HPV) has dramatically reduced incidence and mortality. Early detection is the most important determinant.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Always perform a speculum exam** in any woman with unexplained vaginal bleeding (especially postcoital) – you can’t miss what you don’t see.
2. **Refer urgently (2-week wait)** if any visible cervical abnormality or persistent symptoms – do not assume young age excludes cancer.
3. **Check renal function and consider USS kidneys** in advanced disease – obstruction is silent until creatinine is very high.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do not prescribe hormonal treatments or NSAIDs** to control bleeding without first ruling out cancer via biopsy.
2. **Do not delay biopsy** – if you see a lesion, a punch biopsy can be done in clinic; waiting for colposcopy can take weeks.
3. **Do not use vaginal gel/lubricant** before speculum exam if cytology is planned – it interferes with HPV testing.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Heavy bleeding (soaking pad <1 hour)
  - Severe pelvic pain not controlled by simple analgesia
  - Fever, chills, or offensive discharge
  - Inability to pass urine (obstruction)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Colposcopy within 2 weeks if abnormal screening result
  - Gynae-oncology clinic after diagnosis – MDT plan within 4 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or single parameter red (e.g. SBP <90, RR >24)
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (sepsis or hypoperfusion)
- **Haemorrhage** not controlled by vaginal packing and tranexamic acid → call gynae-oncology reg + interventional radiology (consider embolisation)
- **Acute kidney injury** (creatinine rise >50% from baseline) → call renal team ± interventional radiology for nephrostomy
- **Airway compromise** (rare – massive tumour bleeding into airway) → crash team

rawMarkdown:
## Cervical Cancer
**One-liner:** HPV-driven malignancy of cervix; presents with abnormal bleeding; preventable by screening, curable if detected early.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive haemorrhage** from tumour erosion into uterine/cervical vessels
  - *Single finding:* active heavy vaginal bleeding + haemodynamic instability (HR >110, SBP <90, capillary refill >2 s)
- **Bilateral ureteric obstruction** causing post-renal acute kidney injury
  - *Single finding:* anuria/oliguria + flank pain + palpable bladder (if complete obstruction) – or incidental raised creatinine + empty bladder on exam
- **Septic shock** from pyometra (infected obstructed endometrial cavity) or tumour necrosis
  - *Single finding:* fever + purulent vaginal discharge + hypotension – must differentiate from simple UTI

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Postcoital bleeding (most specific)
  - Intermenstrual bleeding / menorrhagia
  - Postmenopausal bleeding (always suspect until proven otherwise)
  - Persistent watery or offensive vaginal discharge
  - Deep dyspareunia
- **Later signs:**
  - Pelvic/back pain (parametrial invasion)
  - Unilateral lower limb oedema (lymphatic obstruction)
  - Haematuria or tenesmus (bladder/bowel invasion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Speculum: friable, exophytic or ulcerated cervical lesion; contact bleeding
  - Bimanual: bulky cervix, reduced mobility, nodular parametrium (fixed → advanced disease)
  - Inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (metastatic)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Cervical ectropion** – non-friable, red ring around os; no contact bleeding
- **Cervical polyp** – pedunculated, smooth, usually benign
- **Endometritis** – no visible lesion; pelvic tenderness, fever, discharge
- **Cervical fibroid** – firm, irregular but non-friable; usually asymptomatic
- **Metastatic disease** (e.g. endometrial, vaginal, bladder) – imaging ± biopsy needed
- **Splitter:** Friable lesion + contact bleeding on speculum → cervical cancer until proven; ectropion does not bleed on touch.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Vaginal speculum exam (visualise cervix)
  - Bimanual pelvic exam (assess stage)
  - Urinalysis (exclude UTI if sepsis suspected)
  - Bedside USS if obstructive symptoms (look for hydronephrosis)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, clotting, CRP
  - Group & save (if heavy bleeding)
  - Beta-hCG (rule out pregnancy – must do before any procedure)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** pelvic MRI (gold standard for local staging – tumour size, parametrial invasion, lymph nodes)
  - **Second-line:** CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (metastatic workup) or PET-CT if advanced
- **Special tests:**
  - Cervical biopsy (punch or LLETZ) – histology is diagnostic
  - HPV DNA testing (triage for colposcopy)
  - Colposcopy with acetic acid/Lugol’s iodine
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Histology shows squamous cell carcinoma (80%) or adenocarcinoma (20%). Staging via FIGO (clinical + imaging): stage I (confined to cervix) → IV (distant mets).
- **Never-miss test:** **Cervical biopsy** of any visible suspicious lesion – do not treat empirically. If no lesion but high suspicion, refer for colposcopy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Massive haemorrhage: call gynae-oncology ± interventional radiology; insert vaginal packing; IV fluids/blood; tranexamic acid; if unstable → emergency radiotherapy or embolisation
  - Suspected pyometra/sepsis: IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav + metronidazole [check local policy]), urgent drainage (dilatation & curettage ± drain)
  - Acute renal failure: catheterise (bladder decompression if retention); bilateral nephrostomy if obstruction confirmed
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Complete staging (MRI pelvis, CT CAP, PET if needed)
  - MDT referral (gynae-oncology, radiology, histopathology)
  - Discuss fertility-sparing options (early stage)
  - Start chemoradiation if advanced (within 4 weeks ideally)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Early stage (IA–IIA): surgery (cone biopsy, simple/extended hysterectomy) or radiotherapy
  - Locally advanced (IIB–IVA): concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin + external beam + brachytherapy)
  - Metastatic (IVB): palliative chemotherapy (cisplatin/carboplatin + paclitaxel ± bevacizumab)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: crystalloid 1 L/8h if maintaining hydration for cisplatin; otherwise oral + IV if needed
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal, unless bowel obstruction or post-procedure NBM
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; more frequent if unstable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (chemoradiation):**
  - Cisplatin 40 mg/m² IV weekly × 5–6 cycles (with pre/post hydration and antiemetics) – **[check local policy]**
  - For metastatic: carboplatin AUC 5 IV + paclitaxel 175 mg/m² IV q3w – **[check local policy]**
- **Haemorrhage:**
  - Tranexamic acid 1 g IV/PO TDS (max 4 g/day)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Cisplatin: avoid if eGFR <40 mL/min (or reduce dose); ensure Mg²+, K+, Ca²+ replete; contraindicated in pregnancy
  - Paclitaxel: premedicate with dexamethasone + antihistamine (anaphylaxis risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cisplatin dose reduction if eGFR <60; consider carboplatin if worse
  - No significant hepatic adjustment for platinum agents
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Cisplatin – **give adequate hydration (1 L normal saline before and after)** to prevent acute kidney injury; never give without checking recent U&E; ototoxicity is irreversible – monitor audiology if high cumulative dose

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Heavy vaginal bleeding** → hypovolaemic shock
  - **Ureteric obstruction** → acute kidney injury (may need nephrostomy)
  - **Pyometra/sepsis** → septic shock
  - **Fistula** (vesicovaginal / rectovaginal) – especially after radiotherapy
- **Chronic:**
  - **Lymphoedema** (lower limb, genital) – due to nodal involvement or radiotherapy
  - **Radiation proctitis/cystitis** – bleeding, tenesmus, frequency
  - **Sexual dysfunction** – vaginal stenosis, dyspareunia, reduced libido
  - **Infertility** – if hysterectomy or pelvic radiation

### Prognosis
- **Stage-dependent:** stage I 5-year survival >90%; stage II 60–80%; stage III 40–50%; stage IV <20%. Screening (smear + HPV) has dramatically reduced incidence and mortality. Early detection is the most important determinant.

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Always perform a speculum exam** in any woman with unexplained vaginal bleeding (especially postcoital) – you can’t miss what you don’t see.
2. **Refer urgently (2-week wait)** if any visible cervical abnormality or persistent symptoms – do not assume young age excludes cancer.
3. **Check renal function and consider USS kidneys** in advanced disease – obstruction is silent until creatinine is very high.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Do not prescribe hormonal treatments or NSAIDs** to control bleeding without first ruling out cancer via biopsy.
2. **Do not delay biopsy** – if you see a lesion, a punch biopsy can be done in clinic; waiting for colposcopy can take weeks.
3. **Do not use vaginal gel/lubricant** before speculum exam if cytology is planned – it interferes with HPV testing.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Heavy bleeding (soaking pad <1 hour)
  - Severe pelvic pain not controlled by simple analgesia
  - Fever, chills, or offensive discharge
  - Inability to pass urine (obstruction)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Colposcopy within 2 weeks if abnormal screening result
  - Gynae-oncology clinic after diagnosis – MDT plan within 4 weeks

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or single parameter red (e.g. SBP <90, RR >24)
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (sepsis or hypoperfusion)
- **Haemorrhage** not controlled by vaginal packing and tranexamic acid → call gynae-oncology reg + interventional radiology (consider embolisation)
- **Acute kidney injury** (creatinine rise >50% from baseline) → call renal team ± interventional radiology for nephrostomy
- **Airway compromise** (rare – massive tumour bleeding into airway) → crash team
